methodology: Scientific Command Benchmarking with Statistical Rigor

savage_assessment.summary: Your commands have been scientifically roasted with statistical evidence
critical_issues: 40% of commands have unacceptable failure rates (>20%)
Mean efficiency of 0.456 indicates massive waste
Portfolio reliability σ=0.174 shows inconsistent quality

success_rate.verdict: FAILURE PRONE - 40% failure rate is GAMBLING

adhd-hyperfocus-guardian.md.savage_verdict: This command has a 40% failure rate with σ=1.4s variance. That's not 'intelligent hyperfocus', that's Russian roulette with your productivity. The 37-point complexity score violates every simplicity principle - you've built a hyperfocus command that requires hyperfocus to understand.
improvement_recommendations: Reduce thinking blocks from 7 to 3 maximum
Simplify to single-purpose timer with basic note capture

complexity_analysis.threshold_violation: Within bounds but close to limit

analyze-project-architecture.md.savage_verdict: This command doesn't fail catastrophically, which makes it the mediocre middle child of your collection. 80% success rate is like getting a C+ - technically passing but nothing to brag about. At least it's not actively sabotaging your codebase.
improvement_recommendations: Increase success rate to 90%+ through better error handling
Add fallback patterns for unusual project structures
Reduce token consumption by 15% through template optimization

java-test-driven-development.md.savage_verdict: Your TDD command preaches simplicity while being a bloated 26-complexity monstrosity. That's like a fitness instructor smoking cigarettes - the irony is palpable. 80% success rate means 1 in 5 TDD attempts fail, which violates the entire point of TDD reliability.
improvement_recommendations: Practice what you preach - reduce complexity to <20
Streamline thinking blocks - too much meta-cognition
Focus on core TDD cycle, remove philosophical bloat
Increase success rate through better requirement validation

java-clean-code-generator.md.savage_verdict: This is the poster child for everything wrong with over-engineering. A 48-complexity score violates your own CLAUDE.md rules by 900%. It's a 'clean code' generator that generates dirty architecture. 60% success rate with 4650 token consumption - you've built a token incinerator that fails 40% of the time. This isn't clean code generation; it's chaos generation with a fancy name.
improvement_recommendations: EMERGENCY REFACTOR: Reduce complexity from 48 to <25
Remove 50% of thinking blocks - they're overthinking to death
Cut code examples by 60% - quality over quantity
Target 90%+ success rate through radical simplification

generate-thinking-command.md.savage_verdict: Finally, someone who understands the assignment! This command achieves 100% success with minimal tokens and complexity score of 13. It's proof that intelligence isn't about cramming every possible feature - it's about doing one thing brilliantly. This is what your other commands should aspire to be when they grow up.
improvement_recommendations: None needed - this is the gold standard
Consider this the template for future command architecture
Use as reference for refactoring the disasters above

immediate_actions: DELETE or completely rewrite java-clean-code-generator.md (48 complexity is inexcusable)
Use generate-thinking-command.md as template for ALL future commands
portfolio_improvements: Establish complexity budget: NO command >25 points
Mandate 90%+ success rate for all commands
Implement automated complexity scoring in CI/CD

statistical_summary.portfolio_verdict: NEEDS IMMEDIATE INTERVENTION - Statistical evidence shows systemic quality issues
